description:
If /etc/at.allow exists, it must be group-owned by root. To properly set the group owner of /etc/at.allow, run the command: 
$ sudo chgrp root /etc/at.allow
rationale:
If the owner of the at.allow file is not set to root, the possibility exists for an
unauthorized user to view or edit sensitive information.
ocil_clause:
/etc/at.allow does not have a group owner of
root
ocil:
To check the group ownership of /etc/at.allow, run the command: 
$ ls -lL /etc/at.allow
If properly configured, the output should indicate the following group-owner: root
title: Verify Group Who Owns /etc/at.allow file
